encounter.setting: Weekly individual supervision — community mental health agency
encounter.referral_context: Doctoral intern (3rd year practicum) presenting a complex clinical case involving a boundary concern. Supervisor is licensed psychologist.

phases.dialogue: I have something uncomfortable to bring up. My client — the one with borderline PD — friended me on Instagram last week. I didn't accept, but she messaged me and said she 'just wanted to follow my journey.' I didn't respond but now she's brought it up twice in session and she seems really hurt.

chart_reveals.label: Presenting scenario
chart_reveals.value: Client (BPD diagnosis) attempted social media contact via Instagram follow request + direct message

chart_reveals.label: Intern's response
chart_reveals.value: Did not accept follow; did not respond to message; did not yet address in session

phases.dialogue: I didn't know what to do in the moment, so I froze. Part of me wanted to respond to explain, but I thought that might make it worse. I'm worried I've damaged the therapeutic alliance. She keeps saying in session that 'I must think I'm better than her.'

chart_reveals.label: Intern's decision-making
chart_reveals.value: Chose non-response to avoid reinforcing contact; did not yet address limit in session

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee affect
chart_reveals.value: Anxious; some shame about handling; motivated for guidance

chart_reveals.label: Client's in-session behavior
chart_reveals.value: Testing rejection sensitivity theme: 'I must think I'm better than her' — characteristic BPD relational pattern

phases.clinician_prompt: What made you freeze — what were you most afraid of getting wrong?

phases.dialogue: I want to address it next session but I don't know how. I don't want to lecture her about boundaries. She'll feel judged and shut down. But I also know I can't just ignore it. Is there a way to talk about it therapeutically, not as a 'rule'?

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee learning goal
chart_reveals.value: Develop framework for addressing boundary limits therapeutically — without shaming the client

chart_reveals.label: Proposed approach
chart_reveals.value: Seeking supervisor guidance on therapeutic framing of the Instagram incident in next session

phases.clinician_prompt: What do you think the client is really seeking through this contact, in terms of her therapy goals?

questions.prompt: According to Integrated Developmental Model (IDM) supervision theory, how should the supervisor respond to this intern's anxiety and uncertainty?

options.A: Provide direct instruction and a scripted response because Stage 1 trainees need high structure and low autonomy
options.B: Step back entirely to preserve the intern's autonomy since over-guidance creates dependency
options.C: Assess the intern's developmental level on this specific competency and calibrate support accordingly — high structure and support for this new situation
options.D: Refer the clinical case to a senior clinician because the intern demonstrated inadequate judgment

questions.explanation: The Integrated Developmental Model (IDM; Stoltenberg, McNeill, & Delworth) asserts that supervisee development is not uniform across all competencies — an intern may be Stage 3 in some domains and Stage 1 in others. Encountering a boundary violation situation with a personality disorder client is likely a Stage 1 experience for this intern: high anxiety, self-focus, motivation, and limited autonomous confidence. At Stage 1, the supervisor provides high structure (guidance, frameworks, modeling) combined with high support (validation, normalization). As competence in this area grows, structure decreases and autonomy increases.

distractor_rationale.A: While this intern demonstrates some Stage 1 features on this specific issue, IDM emphasizes competency-specific assessment, not a global Stage 1 label. Scripted responses without rationale also limit learning.
distractor_rationale.B: Withdrawing support entirely is appropriate at Stage 3 (masterful, autonomous) — not for a supervisee presenting with high anxiety and limited framework for this situation.
distractor_rationale.D: Referring the case would be abandoning the supervisory role and denying the intern a crucial learning opportunity. Supervision exists precisely for these situations.

questions.prompt: How should the intern address the Instagram incident therapeutically in the next session with the BPD client?

options.A: Avoid raising it directly and redirect whenever the client mentions it, to prevent reinforcing the behavior
options.B: Bring it up first, acknowledge the client's wish for connection, and use it therapeutically to explore the relationship and limit-setting themes
options.C: Provide a formal verbal warning that further contact attempts will result in termination
options.D: Accept the follow request to preserve the therapeutic alliance and then discuss appropriate boundaries afterward

questions.explanation: With BPD clients, limit-setting is most effective when it is therapeutically integrated — not delivered as a rule violation. The intern should proactively bring up the Instagram contact, validate the client's underlying wish for connection ('I noticed you reached out — I want to understand what that was about for you'), set the limit clearly without shaming ('I keep our relationship in the therapy space'), and use the interaction as material for therapy ('This might connect to things you've talked about — feeling close to people and worried about rejection'). This approach maintains alliance, addresses the boundary, and turns a potential rupture into therapeutic work.

distractor_rationale.A: Avoiding the incident allows it to fester and communicates that the topic is too dangerous to discuss — reinforcing the client's rejection fears and abandoning a valuable therapeutic opportunity.
distractor_rationale.C: A formal warning framing would be experienced as punitive by a BPD client, likely triggering abandonment fears and destabilizing the therapeutic relationship without therapeutic benefit.
distractor_rationale.D: Accepting the follow request would constitute a boundary violation — it blurs professional and personal roles, creates dual relationship concerns, and would significantly complicate treatment.

encounter.setting: EAP (Employee Assistance Program) consultation — voluntary self-referral
encounter.referral_context: Licensed clinical psychologist, 12 years post-licensure, self-referred. Reports 'feeling like a different person' over the past year.

phases.dialogue: I don't feel anything anymore. About my clients, I mean. I go in, I do the sessions, I write my notes, I go home. I used to care deeply about this work. Now I sit across from someone in crisis and I'm... calculating the time until session ends. I hate that I do that.

chart_reveals.label: Primary concern
chart_reveals.value: Emotional detachment from clinical work; loss of empathy; time-watching in sessions

chart_reveals.label: Duration
chart_reveals.value: ~12 months of progressive disengagement; no acute precipitant identified

phases.dialogue: After COVID I took on more clients because the need was so great. My caseload went from 25 to 45. I haven't taken a real vacation in three years. My supervision peer group dissolved. I've been isolated professionally. And personally — my marriage is under strain. My wife says I'm 'emotionally absent.'

chart_reveals.label: Work context
chart_reveals.value: Caseload expanded from 25 to 45 post-COVID; no vacation in 3 years; peer consultation discontinued

chart_reveals.label: Personal impact
chart_reveals.value: Marital strain — spouse reports emotional absence; social withdrawal

chart_reveals.label: Professional history
chart_reveals.value: 12 years licensed; previously high satisfaction; no prior burnout episodes

phases.clinician_prompt: When did you first notice that something felt different in your work?

phases.dialogue: I'm worried about my clients. I'm not providing the same quality of care. I'm more irritable in sessions — I can feel it. Last week I cut off a client mid-sentence. I'm ashamed of that. But I don't know how to stop. I can't just close my practice — people depend on me.

chart_reveals.label: Affect
chart_reveals.value: Flat to distressed; shame-laden; appropriate to content

chart_reveals.label: Insight
chart_reveals.value: Excellent — identifies impact on clinical competence; motivated to change

chart_reveals.label: Ethical awareness
chart_reveals.value: Self-identifies potential APA Ethics Code concern (Principle A: Beneficence — competent care)

phases.clinician_prompt: You mentioned worrying about your clients' care. Can you say more about what you've noticed?

questions.prompt: Using the Maslach Burnout Inventory (MBI) framework, which burnout dimension does this psychologist's presentation most comprehensively illustrate?

options.A: Emotional exhaustion only — he reports feeling depleted and unable to give emotionally to his work
options.B: Depersonalization only — his detachment and time-watching indicate cynicism toward clients
options.C: All three MBI dimensions — emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and reduced personal accomplishment
options.D: Reduced personal accomplishment only — his competence concerns suggest declining self-efficacy

questions.explanation: The Maslach Burnout Inventory assesses three dimensions: (1) Emotional Exhaustion — feeling emotionally depleted and drained by one's work ('I don't feel anything anymore'); (2) Depersonalization — cynical, callous, or detached responses to recipients of one's service ('calculating the time until session ends,' cutting off clients, reduced empathy); (3) Reduced Personal Accomplishment — feelings of incompetence and low achievement in one's work ('I'm not providing the same quality of care,' shame, loss of prior satisfaction). This clinician demonstrates all three dimensions, indicating full burnout syndrome.

distractor_rationale.A: Emotional exhaustion alone describes one MBI dimension. This clinician also clearly demonstrates depersonalization and reduced personal accomplishment — all three dimensions are present.
distractor_rationale.B: Depersonalization alone does not capture the full picture. His emotional depletion and explicit competence concerns map onto the other two MBI dimensions.
distractor_rationale.D: Reduced personal accomplishment describes competence concerns and loss of satisfaction. The clinician also demonstrates profound emotional exhaustion (12-month progressive decline, no vacations) and depersonalization (emotional detachment from clients).

questions.prompt: The psychologist expresses concern about his clinical competence but says he cannot stop seeing clients because 'people depend on him.' Under the APA Ethics Code, what is the most appropriate response?

options.A: Support his decision to continue working at full capacity while teaching stress management strategies
options.B: Immediately mandate that he cease all clinical practice until burnout is fully resolved
options.C: Acknowledge the ethical tension, explore immediate caseload reduction, and address the 'indispensable' belief as a maintaining factor
options.D: Report him to the state licensing board for practicing outside his current competence

questions.explanation: APA Ethics Code Principle A (Beneficence and Nonmaleficence) and Standard 2.06 (Personal Problems and Conflicts) require psychologists to recognize when personal difficulties are compromising competent service delivery and to take appropriate action. However, the immediate therapeutic response is not reporting or mandated cessation — it is collaborative ethical exploration. The belief that he is 'indispensable' is a cognitive distortion that maintains the unsafe caseload and reflects a common burnout feature (over-responsibility). The consultation should address: immediate caseload reduction, return to peer supervision/consultation, and possibly personal therapy — while planning thoughtful transitions for client welfare rather than abrupt abandonment.

distractor_rationale.A: Continuing at full capacity while adding stress management ignores the active ethical concern (compromised competence) and the unsustainable systemic factors. Stress management alone is insufficient.
distractor_rationale.B: Immediate cessation of all clinical practice without a transition plan would constitute abandonment (APA Standard 3.12) of clients who depend on continuity of care. An abrupt stop also fails to address the organizational and cognitive factors driving burnout.
distractor_rationale.D: EAP consultation is a protected voluntary help-seeking resource. Reporting a voluntary self-referral to a licensing board would violate the confidentiality of that consultation and would deter future help-seeking among impaired professionals — producing worse outcomes at a systems level.

encounter.setting: Supervision office in a community mental health center
encounter.referral_context: Regularly scheduled individual supervision session. Supervisee is a second-year doctoral practicum student who requested extra time this week to discuss a difficult case.

phases.dialogue: I'm really glad we could meet today. So I've been seeing this client — mid-thirties, came in for depression — and last session she disclosed that she's been having thoughts about ending her life. Not like a plan exactly, but she said something like, 'Sometimes I just think everyone would be better off.' I froze. I asked a couple of follow-up questions but honestly I don't think I handled it well at all. I've been anxious about it all weekend.

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee concern
chart_reveals.value: Uncertainty about handling client's suicidal ideation disclosure

chart_reveals.label: Client presentation
chart_reveals.value: Female client, mid-30s, presenting for depression; disclosed passive suicidal ideation in most recent session

phases.dialogue: So after she said it, I kind of just paused and then asked if she was safe. She said yeah, she's fine, it's just thoughts. I didn't do a formal risk assessment — I know I should have. I didn't ask about means or a plan or anything like that. I just… I think I was scared of making it worse? Like if I asked too many questions she'd shut down. I documented that she denied active SI but honestly I feel like I didn't get enough information to really know.

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee's response to disclosure
chart_reveals.value: Asked if client was safe; client denied active SI; no formal risk assessment conducted

chart_reveals.label: Documentation
chart_reveals.value: Supervisee documented denial of active SI but acknowledges assessment was insufficient

phases.clinician_prompt: Walk me through exactly what happened after she made that statement. What did you say, and what did she say back?

phases.phase_label: Supervisee's Training Context and Developmental Needs
phases.dialogue: In my program we covered suicide assessment in our ethics class, and I've read about the Columbia Protocol, but I've never actually had someone say something like that to me in session before. It's totally different when it's real. I guess I'm also worried — am I in trouble for not doing the full assessment? I want to do this right. I just need help figuring out what to do next and how to handle it if it comes up again.

chart_reveals.label: Training level
chart_reveals.value: Second-year doctoral practicum student; didactic exposure to suicide assessment but no prior clinical experience with SI disclosure

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee knowledge base
chart_reveals.value: Familiar with Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) from coursework; first live encounter with suicidal ideation

phases.clinician_prompt: What training have you had on suicide risk assessment so far, and how are you feeling about where to go from here?

questions.prompt: As the clinical supervisor, what is the most appropriate immediate action to take in this supervision session?

options.A: Reassure the supervisee that passive SI is low-risk and no further action is needed until the next client session
options.B: Instruct the supervisee to contact the client before the next session to conduct a phone-based risk assessment, and collaboratively develop a plan for structured suicide risk assessment going forward
options.C: Remove the supervisee from the case due to the clinical error and assign a more experienced trainee
options.D: File an incident report about the supervisee's failure to conduct a proper risk assessment

questions.explanation: The most appropriate immediate action combines ensuring client safety (contacting the client to assess current risk level) with a supervisory teaching function (collaboratively developing a structured approach for future suicide risk assessment). This balances the dual responsibilities of supervision: gatekeeping for client welfare and facilitating supervisee professional development.

distractor_rationale.A: Dismissing the need for follow-up based on the characterization of passive SI is clinically inappropriate, as the supervisee acknowledged the assessment was incomplete. The supervisor has a duty to ensure adequate risk assessment occurs.
distractor_rationale.C: Removing the supervisee from the case would be premature and punitive. A developmentally appropriate supervisory response involves teaching through the experience, not avoiding it. Case removal would only be warranted if the supervisee demonstrated persistent incompetence or the client required a level of care beyond the supervisee's capacity even with close supervision.
distractor_rationale.D: Filing an incident report at this stage is disproportionate. The supervisee is a trainee who identified the gap and proactively sought supervision. The appropriate response is corrective guidance and enhanced oversight, not punitive documentation.

encounter.setting: Conference room in a pediatric primary care clinic
encounter.referral_context: A pediatrician has requested an organizational consultation from a psychologist embedded in the integrated care team. The pediatrician is concerned about how behavioral health referrals are being handled and wants guidance on improving the workflow.

patient.label: Adult Male, 44
appearance_tags: reading glasses pushed up on forehead

phases.dialogue: Thanks for sitting down with me. Look, I'll be blunt — the referral process is broken. I'm screening kids with the PSC-17 like we agreed, and I'm flagging cases, but then what? I put in the referral and families wait three, four weeks before anyone from your side reaches out. By then half of them have given up. I had a mom last week in tears because her eight-year-old is having panic attacks at school and she was told the earliest appointment was six weeks out. I feel like I'm doing my part but the system is failing these families.

chart_reveals.label: Consultee concern
chart_reveals.value: Frustration with lengthy wait times for behavioral health referrals in pediatric integrated care setting

chart_reveals.label: Current workflow
chart_reveals.value: PSC-17 screening in place; referral-to-contact lag of 3–6 weeks; significant patient attrition

phases.dialogue: It's been like this for about six months now, ever since we lost one of the behavioral health clinicians who left for private practice. We never backfilled the position. Before that, the warm handoff model was actually working pretty well — I'd flag a kid, walk the family down the hall, and they'd see someone that day or the next. Now it's all phone-based scheduling through a central intake line, and honestly I think that's where everything falls apart. The families don't even understand who they're supposed to call. And I don't feel comfortable just telling a parent 'call this number' when their kid is struggling.

chart_reveals.label: Timeline
chart_reveals.value: Workflow deteriorated ~6 months ago after departure of one BH clinician; position not backfilled

chart_reveals.label: Prior model
chart_reveals.value: Previously used same-day warm handoff model successfully; current model relies on centralized phone intake

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you help me understand how the referral process has changed over time? Was there a point when it was working better?

phases.dialogue: Honestly? Part of what makes this hard is I don't feel like anyone on the admin side is listening. I brought this up at the last staff meeting and the clinic director basically said we all need to be patient while they work on hiring. But that was three months ago and nothing's changed. I'm not trying to step on anyone's toes — I know you all are stretched thin too. I just want to figure out if there's something we can do right now, even before they hire someone, so these kids aren't falling through the cracks. I became a pediatrician to help kids, not to hand parents a phone number and hope for the best.

chart_reveals.label: Organizational dynamics
chart_reveals.value: Consultee feels unheard by administration; clinic director has not acted on staffing concern raised 3 months ago

chart_reveals.label: Consultee motivation
chart_reveals.value: Seeking interim solutions; open to collaboration; values integrated care model

phases.clinician_prompt: It sounds like you've tried to raise this before. What kind of support are you getting from clinic leadership, and what would an ideal next step look like from your perspective?

phases.dialogue: I actually pulled some numbers. Out of the last forty referrals I made, only about fifteen families actually completed an intake appointment. That's not even half. And I can tell you from my end, probably twenty of those forty were kids who really needed to be seen — anxiety, behavioral issues, one kid I was pretty worried about for ADHD plus depression. Fifteen out of forty is not acceptable. If you can help me put together something to show admin — real data — maybe that gets us somewhere.

chart_reveals.label: Referral completion rate
chart_reveals.value: 15/40 (37.5%) referrals completed intake in recent tracking period

chart_reveals.label: Clinical acuity
chart_reveals.value: Consultee estimates ~50% of referred cases involve significant clinical need (anxiety, behavioral disorders, comorbid ADHD/depression)

phases.clinician_prompt: Do you have any data on how many of those referrals actually result in completed appointments?

questions.prompt: As the consulting psychologist, what is the most appropriate initial recommendation to address the referral gap in this integrated care setting?

options.A: Advise the pediatrician to stop screening with the PSC-17 until the staffing issue is resolved to avoid identifying needs that cannot be met
options.B: Recommend reinstating a modified warm handoff protocol using existing behavioral health staff for high-acuity cases while collaborating with the consultee to present utilization data to administration advocating for position backfill
options.C: Suggest the pediatrician manage mild-to-moderate behavioral health cases independently to reduce the referral burden
options.D: Recommend the consultee formally complain to the hospital board about the clinic director's inaction

questions.explanation: The most effective consultation response addresses both the immediate clinical gap and the systemic issue. Reinstating a modified warm handoff for high-acuity cases uses existing resources to triage effectively, while leveraging the consultee's data to advocate to administration addresses the root cause. This reflects best practices in organizational consultation: collaborative problem-solving, evidence-based advocacy, and tiered intervention.

distractor_rationale.A: Discontinuing screening would be ethically problematic and clinically harmful. Universal screening is a best practice in pediatric integrated care, and stopping it does not solve the access problem — it merely hides it.
distractor_rationale.C: While brief interventions by pediatricians can be appropriate, suggesting independent management of behavioral health cases exceeds the consultee's scope of practice and training, and does not address the systemic workflow issue.
distractor_rationale.D: Escalating directly to a hospital board bypasses appropriate organizational channels and could damage the consultee's professional relationships. Effective consultation involves working within existing structures first, using data to support advocacy through appropriate leadership channels.

questions.prompt: The consultee mentions that many of the families who fail to complete the referral process are Spanish-speaking families. Which of the following consultation recommendations best addresses this disparity?

options.A: Suggest that Spanish-speaking families are likely less interested in behavioral health services due to cultural stigma and should not be prioritized for outreach
options.B: Recommend that the pediatrician provide families with Spanish-language psychoeducational materials and consider that as sufficient intervention
options.C: Advise evaluating the centralized intake process for language access barriers, including availability of bilingual staff and interpreter services, and develop a culturally responsive outreach protocol for follow-up
options.D: Recommend referring all Spanish-speaking families to external community organizations instead of the clinic's behavioral health services

questions.explanation: A systems-level consultation approach requires examining structural barriers to access, including language accessibility at each point of the referral pathway. Evaluating the intake process for language barriers and developing culturally responsive outreach directly addresses the disparity while maintaining the integrated care model's intent to serve all families equitably.

distractor_rationale.A: This reflects a culturally biased assumption. Research shows that access barriers — not lack of interest — are the primary driver of disparities in behavioral health service utilization among Latino/a families. Stereotyping a population's preferences is inappropriate in consultation.
distractor_rationale.B: While psychoeducational materials in Spanish are helpful, they do not address the systemic intake barrier. Providing written materials alone is insufficient when the referral process itself is inaccessible.
distractor_rationale.D: Diverting an entire population to external resources rather than addressing internal access barriers constitutes a form of institutional exclusion. The consultation goal should be to make the existing system equitable, not to redirect underserved populations away from it.

encounter.setting: Virtual supervision session via secure telehealth platform at a university counseling center
encounter.referral_context: Scheduled weekly supervision session. The supervisee is a postdoctoral fellow in a university counseling center who has been working with a graduate student client presenting with significant anxiety. The supervisee wants to discuss a diagnostic question.

patient.label: Adult Male, 31
appearance_tags: visible on webcam from home office

phases.dialogue: Hey, thanks for making time. So I've been seeing this grad student — K.R., 24, came in about two months ago saying she was overwhelmed with her program. Typical grad school stress, I thought at first. But the more I work with her, the less sure I am about what's really going on. She meets with me weekly, and some sessions she's super productive and engaged, and other sessions she's completely shut down, almost dissociated. Last week she told me she sometimes feels like she's watching herself from outside her body during seminars. That caught my attention. I'm not sure if this is GAD that's getting worse, or if there's something else I should be considering.

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee concern
chart_reveals.value: Diagnostic uncertainty regarding client K.R., 24-year-old graduate student presenting with anxiety and dissociative symptoms

chart_reveals.label: Client presentation
chart_reveals.value: 2-month treatment; initial presentation of academic stress/overwhelm; variable session engagement; reports depersonalization during seminars

phases.phase_label: Client History as Reported by Supervisee
phases.dialogue: So what I've gathered so far — K.R. grew up in a pretty strict household. Her parents were both professionals, very high expectations. She mentioned offhand once that her father had a temper, and when I followed up gently she got really quiet and changed the subject. She's never used the word 'abuse' but there are pieces that make me wonder. She said she's always been a worrier, always felt like she had to be perfect or something terrible would happen. The dissociation thing — she says it's been happening since college, maybe earlier, but it's gotten worse since starting her doctoral program. She also told me she sometimes loses chunks of time, like she'll be driving home and not remember the drive. That's what really made me think this might be more than GAD.

chart_reveals.label: Dissociative symptoms
chart_reveals.value: Depersonalization during seminars; time loss (e.g., driving without memory of the trip); onset in college or earlier, worsening in doctoral program

chart_reveals.label: Developmental history
chart_reveals.value: Strict, high-expectation household; father described as having a 'temper'; possible undisclosed abuse; client deflects when topic arises

chart_reveals.label: Prior diagnosis
chart_reveals.value: No prior mental health treatment; no previous diagnoses

phases.clinician_prompt: Tell me more about what you know about K.R.'s history. Has she shared anything about her background or when these experiences started?

phases.phase_label: Supervisee's Observations of Client Mental Status
phases.dialogue: Mental status is honestly what's confusing me. When she's 'on,' she's articulate, insightful, makes great eye contact. Her affect matches what she's talking about. But when she flips into that other mode — and it can happen mid-session — her eyes kind of glaze, her voice goes flat, and she seems younger, almost? Like her vocabulary simplifies. The first time it happened I honestly didn't know what to do. I just sat with her for a minute and then gently said her name, and she sort of came back. She apologized and said 'that happens sometimes.' She didn't seem distressed about it, more resigned. I noted her orientation was intact both times, no evidence of psychosis, judgment and insight are generally good when she's present.
behavioral_tags: voice dropping when describing dissociative episodes

chart_reveals.label: Variable presentation
chart_reveals.value: Alternates between engaged/articulate and flat/dissociated states, sometimes within a single session

chart_reveals.label: Dissociative features
chart_reveals.value: Eyes glaze, flat voice, age-regressed quality during dissociative episodes; oriented x4; no psychotic features; insight/judgment intact when present

chart_reveals.label: Client's self-report
chart_reveals.value: Client reports episodes are chronic and ego-syntonic ('that happens sometimes'); appears resigned rather than distressed

phases.clinician_prompt: When you observe K.R. in session, what does her mental status look like — especially during those moments when she shuts down?

phases.dialogue: So here's where I'm stuck. I initially conceptualized this as GAD — the chronic worry, the perfectionism, the somatic tension she mentions. And I started CBT-based work. But now with the dissociation, the possible trauma history, the time loss — I'm wondering if I should be thinking about a dissociative disorder, or maybe PTSD. Or could it be both? I guess what I need from you is help thinking through the differential and whether I need to shift my treatment approach. Also — and I want to be honest — I've never treated a dissociative disorder before. If that's what this is, I want to make sure I'm competent to handle it or if we need to think about a referral.

chart_reveals.label: Current treatment approach
chart_reveals.value: CBT for GAD initiated; supervisee questioning appropriateness given emerging dissociative features

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee self-assessment
chart_reveals.value: No prior experience treating dissociative disorders; proactively raising competence concern; open to referral if indicated

phases.clinician_prompt: Given everything you've described, what diagnostic possibilities are you considering, and how are you thinking about next steps?

questions.prompt: Based on the supervisee's description of client K.R.'s symptoms — including depersonalization, time loss, variable in-session presentation with apparent identity shifts, and possible childhood trauma — which DSM-5-TR diagnosis should the supervisor guide the supervisee to evaluate most carefully?

options.A: Generalized Anxiety Disorder
options.B: Dissociative Identity Disorder
options.C: Posttraumatic Stress Disorder
options.D: Depersonalization/Derealization Disorder

questions.explanation: The combination of depersonalization, dissociative amnesia (time loss), and apparent shifts in identity states during sessions (including changes in voice, vocabulary, and apparent age) is most consistent with Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID) per DSM-5-TR criteria. DID requires disruption of identity characterized by two or more distinct personality states, recurrent gaps in recall, and clinically significant distress or impairment. The probable childhood trauma history is consistent with the established etiology of DID.

distractor_rationale.A: While K.R. has anxiety symptoms and chronic worry consistent with GAD, GAD does not account for dissociative amnesia, depersonalization, or the identity shifts observed in session. GAD may be a comorbid condition but does not capture the primary clinical picture.
distractor_rationale.C: PTSD could account for dissociative features (the dissociative subtype includes depersonalization and derealization), but PTSD does not typically present with distinct identity states or the level of dissociative amnesia described (e.g., losing time while driving). The in-session shifts suggesting different identity states point more specifically toward DID.
distractor_rationale.D: Depersonalization/Derealization Disorder involves persistent depersonalization or derealization but requires that identity disruption and dissociative amnesia are absent. K.R.'s time loss and apparent identity shifts exceed the criteria for this diagnosis.

questions.prompt: The supervisee has openly acknowledged having no prior experience treating dissociative disorders. As the supervisor, what is the most appropriate immediate supervisory response?

options.A: Immediately transfer the client to a clinician with dissociative disorder expertise to protect the client from potential harm
options.B: Affirm the supervisee's self-awareness and ethical reasoning, then collaboratively develop a plan that includes structured assessment, enhanced supervision, and identification of a consultation resource with dissociative disorder expertise while maintaining the therapeutic relationship
options.C: Reassure the supervisee that a postdoctoral fellow should be competent to treat any diagnosis and encourage continued CBT as originally planned
options.D: Instruct the supervisee to avoid exploring trauma-related content to prevent iatrogenic harm until the diagnostic picture is clearer

questions.explanation: The best supervisory response balances multiple ethical obligations: protecting the client through enhanced oversight, respecting the therapeutic alliance already established, supporting the supervisee's professional development, and ensuring competent care through structured assessment and expert consultation. Abruptly transferring the client could be destabilizing, while ignoring the competence gap or restricting clinical exploration would be equally problematic.

distractor_rationale.A: While a referral may ultimately be warranted, immediately transferring the client without adequate assessment or transition planning could be destabilizing for a client with a dissociative disorder. The established therapeutic relationship has value, and the decision to transfer should be made thoughtfully after further evaluation, not reflexively.
distractor_rationale.C: Dismissing the supervisee's competence concern is a supervisory failure. Ethical practice requires accurate self-assessment, and the supervisee is demonstrating appropriate professional behavior. Continuing an unmodified CBT approach without addressing dissociative features could be ineffective or harmful.
distractor_rationale.D: While pacing of trauma exploration is important, categorically avoiding trauma content is not consistent with evidence-based approaches to dissociative disorders. Stabilization-focused work is appropriate, but an avoidance-based directive does not constitute adequate supervision and could reinforce the supervisee's anxiety about addressing the clinical material.

encounter.setting: Supervision office in a university counseling center
encounter.referral_context: A postdoctoral fellow requests an unscheduled supervision meeting, appearing visibly upset about an ongoing case and her own professional response to it.

phases.dialogue: I need to talk to you about something before my next session this afternoon. I have a client — a grad student dealing with severe anxiety and some passive suicidal ideation — and I realized last night that I've been… I don't know, I think I've been avoiding the suicide risk piece. Like actively steering conversations away from it. And I don't know if it's because I'm not competent enough to handle it or if something else is going on with me.

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee concern
chart_reveals.value: Postdoctoral fellow recognizes avoidance of suicide risk assessment with an active client

chart_reveals.label: Client risk level
chart_reveals.value: Client reportedly presenting with passive suicidal ideation alongside severe anxiety

phases.dialogue: My training program covered suicide risk assessment — I did the workshops, I know the Columbia Protocol, I've done safety planning before. But this client… she reminds me of my college roommate. My roommate attempted our junior year and I was the one who found her. I thought I had dealt with all that. I went to my own therapy for over a year. But sitting across from this client, hearing her describe that hollow feeling — it's like my body just shuts down and I redirect to the anxiety stuff.
behavioral_tags: breaks eye contact when discussing roommate

chart_reveals.value: Completed formal suicide risk assessment training including Columbia Protocol; has prior safety planning experience

chart_reveals.label: Personal history
chart_reveals.value: Supervisee's college roommate made a suicide attempt; supervisee discovered her; received personal therapy for over one year

chart_reveals.label: Countertransference trigger
chart_reveals.value: Current client's presentation activates unresolved trauma response related to roommate's attempt

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you help me understand more about what you think might be driving the avoidance? You mentioned competence, but it sounds like there might be something more personal.

phases.dialogue: Honestly, the rest of my caseload is fine. I feel solid with my other clients. It's just this one. But I've been losing sleep over it — not just worry about the client, but this awful guilt. Like what if something happens to her because I couldn't do my job? And then I start spiraling about whether I'm even cut out for this field. My partner keeps telling me I'm being too hard on myself, but she doesn't really get it. This isn't about perfectionism. Someone could get hurt.

chart_reveals.label: Functioning with other clients
chart_reveals.value: Reports feeling competent and effective with remainder of caseload

chart_reveals.label: Sleep and distress

phases.clinician_prompt: How is this affecting the rest of your work and your life outside the office?

phases.dialogue: I know I need to do the risk assessment with her today. I know that. Part of me wants to ask you to transfer her, but I also know that running from it doesn't fix what's going on with me. I just — I need you to tell me what the right move is here. Do I keep seeing her? Do I tell her that I've been dropping the ball? I feel like every option has something wrong with it.

chart_reveals.label: Supervisee's self-assessment
chart_reveals.value: Recognizes need for immediate risk assessment; ambivalent about case transfer vs. continued treatment

chart_reveals.label: Ethical awareness
chart_reveals.value: Aware of potential competence limitation and client safety concern; seeking supervisory guidance

phases.clinician_prompt: What do you think the options are, and what feels most important to you right now?

questions.prompt: Based on the full supervision encounter, what is the most accurate formulation of the primary issue affecting this supervisee's professional functioning?

options.A: General clinical incompetence requiring remediation of suicide risk assessment skills
options.B: Countertransference reaction triggered by unresolved personal trauma, creating a circumscribed competence impairment
options.C: Professional burnout with depersonalization affecting her entire caseload
options.D: Vicarious traumatization from accumulated exposure to suicidal clients

questions.explanation: The supervisee demonstrates adequate training and competence across her caseload, with avoidance limited to one client whose presentation mirrors her own traumatic experience. This is a countertransference reaction rooted in personal trauma that creates a specific, circumscribed impairment — not a global skills deficit, burnout syndrome, or vicarious traumatization from cumulative exposure.

distractor_rationale.A: The supervisee has completed formal risk assessment training and performs well with all other clients, ruling out a generalized competence deficit.
distractor_rationale.C: Burnout typically involves emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and reduced efficacy across professional functioning. Her difficulty is isolated to one client and one clinical domain.
distractor_rationale.D: Vicarious traumatization involves cumulative effects from exposure to multiple clients' trauma material over time. Her reaction is specific to one client who triggers a personal trauma memory.

questions.prompt: As the supervisor, what is the most appropriate immediate course of action?

options.A: Transfer the client immediately to another clinician to eliminate the risk of harm
options.B: Conduct a joint session with the supervisee to complete the risk assessment today, develop a plan for the supervisee to return to personal therapy, and reassess case assignment
options.C: Reassure the supervisee that her reaction is normal and encourage her to push through the discomfort in her next session
options.D: File a formal competence concern with the training director and remove the supervisee from clinical duties pending review

questions.explanation: The most appropriate immediate action balances client safety with supervisee development. Conducting a joint session ensures the risk assessment happens today while modeling the skill in vivo. Recommending a return to personal therapy addresses the underlying countertransference, and reassessing case assignment allows a thoughtful rather than reactive decision about ongoing care.

distractor_rationale.A: Immediate transfer addresses client safety but misses the developmental opportunity and may reinforce the supervisee's avoidance. It should be considered only if the supervisee cannot participate in a joint session or if reassessment indicates ongoing impairment.
distractor_rationale.C: Simply reassuring and encouraging the supervisee to push through minimizes the seriousness of the avoidance and does not ensure client safety regarding the overdue risk assessment.
distractor_rationale.D: A formal competence concern is premature. The supervisee self-identified the problem, sought supervision proactively, and demonstrates insight. This is within the expected range of supervisory work, not a remediation-level concern.

encounter.setting: Conference room at a community mental health agency during an organizational consultation
encounter.referral_context: The clinical director of a community mental health center has retained an external psychologist-consultant to address escalating interpersonal conflict among the clinical staff that is impacting client care and retention.

phases.dialogue: Thanks for coming in. Look, I'll be blunt — I'm losing staff and I can't afford to lose any more. In the last four months, three clinicians have resigned. The two senior therapists who are left won't speak to each other, the newer staff are caught in the middle picking sides, and last week a client filed a formal complaint because she showed up for her appointment and her therapist had quit without anyone telling her. I've tried team meetings, I've tried talking to people individually. Nothing sticks. I'm starting to think the whole culture here is broken.

chart_reveals.label: Presenting problem
chart_reveals.value: Escalating staff conflict, three clinician resignations in four months, client care disruption

chart_reveals.label: Client impact
chart_reveals.value: Formal client complaint due to unmanaged clinician departure; continuity of care breakdown

chart_reveals.label: Prior interventions
chart_reveals.value: Team meetings and individual conversations attempted without sustained improvement

phases.dialogue: We've always been a tight-knit place — that was our identity. About eight months ago, the agency got a big grant to expand services into substance use treatment. I was thrilled. But I brought in a new program coordinator, Dr. M., who came from a hospital system. She's brilliant clinically, but she started implementing all these new protocols — standardized outcome measures, structured treatment manuals, productivity benchmarks. The senior staff felt like she was saying they'd been doing it wrong for years. And honestly? I backed Dr. M. because the data matters, especially with the grant. I think that's when people started feeling like I'd abandoned them.
behavioral_tags: defensive posture when discussing his decisions

chart_reveals.label: Change implementation approach

chart_reveals.label: Leadership dynamic
chart_reveals.value: Clinical director aligned with new coordinator; existing senior staff perceived this as devaluation of their prior work

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you walk me through what changed? Was there a specific event or shift that seemed to set things off?

phases.dialogue: Here's what really worries me. The two senior therapists — R.K. and T.L. — used to be close. R.K. was more open to the changes and started working with Dr. M. on the substance use protocols. T.L. saw that as a betrayal. Now T.L. has basically organized an informal resistance — she eats lunch with the newer staff, vents about how 'corporate' everything has become, and I've heard she's been telling people that Dr. M. is going to get everyone replaced with cheaper hires. I don't think that's true, but the fear is real. And the three people who left? All of them were closer to T.L. Meanwhile, Dr. M. came to me last week saying she feels undermined and unsupported, and she's considering leaving too. If she goes, we lose the grant infrastructure.
behavioral_tags: long pause before naming T.L.'s behavior

chart_reveals.label: Faction dynamics
chart_reveals.value: Staff split into pro-change (aligned with Dr. M. and R.K.) and resistant (aligned with T.L.) factions

chart_reveals.label: Informal influence
chart_reveals.value: T.L. actively organizing resistance among newer staff; spreading fears about job security

chart_reveals.label: Attrition pattern
chart_reveals.value: Three departed clinicians were aligned with T.L.; Dr. M. now threatening to leave due to feeling undermined

chart_reveals.label: Organizational stakes
chart_reveals.value: Loss of Dr. M. would jeopardize grant-funded program infrastructure

phases.clinician_prompt: Tell me more about the interpersonal dynamics. Who are the key players and how are the lines drawn?

phases.dialogue: I pulled some numbers before you came. Client no-show rates have gone up 18% in the last quarter. Staff satisfaction surveys — we do them annually — showed a 30-point drop in 'feeling valued by leadership.' And here's the thing that keeps me up at night: our client outcomes on the new measures are actually improving for the substance use track. The system works. But it doesn't matter if there's nobody left to run it.

phases.clinician_prompt: Do you have any data — satisfaction surveys, productivity reports, client outcome metrics — that might help me see the bigger picture?

questions.prompt: Which organizational behavior framework best accounts for the pattern of dysfunction described in this consultation?

options.A: Role ambiguity and job-person mismatch, where staff lack clarity about their responsibilities under the new structure
options.B: Organizational change resistance driven by procedural injustice — staff perceive the change process as imposed without voice or participation, leading to in-group/out-group polarization
options.C: Leader-member exchange (LMX) deterioration caused primarily by the clinical director's personality-based favoritism toward Dr. M.
options.D: Structural reorganization failure where the agency lacks the administrative capacity to absorb a new service line

questions.explanation: The core organizational dynamic is a failed change management process characterized by procedural injustice. Substantive changes were imposed without adequate staff participation or buy-in, leading to perceptions that existing staff contributions were devalued. This triggered in-group/out-group polarization (pro-change vs. resistant factions), informal resistance leadership, and cascading attrition — all consistent with organizational justice theory and change resistance literature.

distractor_rationale.A: While some role confusion may exist, the primary complaints center on how changes were implemented and perceived devaluation of staff, not unclear job descriptions or poor person-job fit.
distractor_rationale.C: While the clinical director's alignment with Dr. M. is relevant, framing this as personality-based favoritism oversimplifies the systemic issue. The director made a strategic decision to support evidence-based changes; the problem is how that decision was communicated and implemented, not inherent favoritism.
distractor_rationale.D: The agency successfully implemented the new service line with improving clinical outcomes. The dysfunction is interpersonal and cultural, not structural or administrative.

questions.prompt: As the organizational consultant, which intervention strategy is most appropriate to recommend as the primary approach?

options.A: Recommend terminating T.L. to eliminate the source of informal resistance and stabilize the team
options.B: Implement a structured organizational development process that includes facilitated dialogue between factions, renegotiation of change implementation to include staff voice, and leadership coaching for the clinical director on managing change
options.C: Advise the clinical director to reverse all new protocols to restore the previous organizational culture and rebuild trust
options.D: Conduct individual coaching sessions with Dr. M. and T.L. to resolve their personal conflict, as the organizational problems will resolve once key individuals reconcile

questions.explanation: A multi-level organizational development approach addresses the systemic nature of the dysfunction. Facilitated dialogue allows airing of grievances and rebuilding trust; renegotiating implementation gives staff voice in the process (restoring procedural justice); and leadership coaching helps the clinical director recognize his role in the dynamic and develop skills for managing future change. This approach treats the organization as the client, not any single individual.

distractor_rationale.A: Terminating T.L. would likely confirm staff fears about the new regime, accelerate attrition, and fail to address the underlying procedural justice issues that created the resistance in the first place.
distractor_rationale.C: Reversing all changes would sacrifice demonstrably effective clinical improvements and undermine the grant-funded program. The goal is to repair the process, not abandon beneficial outcomes.
distractor_rationale.D: While individual coaching may be a component, framing this as a personal conflict between two individuals ignores the systemic dynamics, the clinical director's leadership role, and the broader staff polarization.

encounter.setting: Virtual peer consultation group meeting via secure telehealth platform
encounter.referral_context: A licensed psychologist in independent practice raises a concern during a monthly peer consultation group about an evolving dual relationship and scope-of-practice issue that has been causing significant professional distress.

patient.label: Adult Male, 52
appearance_tags: reading glasses pushed up on forehead

phases.dialogue: I appreciate you all letting me take the floor. I've been sitting on this for about three weeks because I wasn't sure it was actually a problem, but it's been eating at me and I think I need outside eyes. I have a long-term therapy client — we've been working together for about two years on complex grief and identity issues. About six weeks ago, my client told me his company is contracting with a consulting firm to do leadership development training, and he's been asked to recommend a psychologist. He asked if I'd be interested. And here's where I made a mistake — I didn't say no immediately. I said I'd think about it.

chart_reveals.label: Presenting concern
chart_reveals.value: Licensed psychologist considering organizational consulting role offered through a current long-term therapy client

chart_reveals.label: Clinical relationship
chart_reveals.value: Two-year therapeutic relationship focused on complex grief and identity issues

chart_reveals.label: Decision delay
chart_reveals.value: Did not decline immediately; told client he would consider the offer approximately 6 weeks ago

phases.dialogue: So what happened next is the part I'm really struggling with. A week later, my client brought a preliminary scope of work document to our therapy session. He was excited — genuinely proud to recommend me. And I — look, I read it. It was a leadership coaching and team assessment contract, exactly the kind of organizational work I've been trying to build my practice around. I've done trainings, I have the competencies, and frankly my practice has been financially tight this year. But as I was reading it, my client started talking about how this could be 'good for both of us,' and I felt my stomach drop. Because suddenly I wasn't sure if this was a professional opportunity or if my client was trying to take care of me.
behavioral_tags: avoids camera eye line when discussing financial pressure
voice slows notably at 'good for both of us'

chart_reveals.label: Boundary progression
chart_reveals.value: Client brought business document to therapy session; clinician reviewed it during session context

chart_reveals.label: Financial context

chart_reveals.label: Relational dynamic concern
chart_reveals.value: Client's framing of opportunity as 'good for both of us' raised concern about caretaking dynamic within therapeutic relationship

phases.clinician_prompt: What happened after you told him you'd think about it?

phases.dialogue: I haven't brought it up again in session, and neither has he — which is its own red flag, right? It's this elephant in the room. Our last two sessions have felt flat. I'm self-monitoring so hard that I can't be present. I keep analyzing every intervention I make — am I being genuine or am I unconsciously trying to preserve the consulting opportunity? And then I think about the fact that if I take the contract, I'd potentially be assessing his colleagues, maybe even his supervisor. I'd have information about his work environment that he's sharing with me as a client, and I'd be walking into that system with a completely different hat on. The boundary lines aren't just blurred — they'd be obliterated.
behavioral_tags: speech becomes more analytical and detached

chart_reveals.label: Therapeutic functioning
chart_reveals.value: Reports impaired therapeutic presence in recent sessions; excessive self-monitoring interfering with clinical work

chart_reveals.label: Insight and judgment
chart_reveals.value: Demonstrates strong ethical reasoning regarding multiple relationship risks, confidentiality conflicts, and role confusion

chart_reveals.label: Identified role conflicts
chart_reveals.value: Would hold confidential therapy information about client while conducting organizational assessment of client's workplace system

phases.clinician_prompt: How has this situation been affecting your actual clinical work with this client?

phases.dialogue: There's one more layer I haven't mentioned. My client's grief work — the core of our therapy — is about his father, who was a business executive. A lot of what we've processed is my client feeling like he could never earn his father's respect professionally. So when he brings me this opportunity and says it would be 'good for both of us,' I can see the transference a mile away. He's trying to be the good son who provides. And the consulting contract is exactly the kind of thing his father would have approved of — bringing a professional in, being the connector. If I take it, I'm playing right into that dynamic. If I decline, I have to be very careful about how that lands for him given his history. And meanwhile, I haven't disclosed any of my financial situation to him, but I'm wondering if he sensed something. He's perceptive.
behavioral_tags: more animated as he formulates the transference analysis
briefly smiles at own clinical insight

chart_reveals.label: Client's core therapeutic themes
chart_reveals.value: Complex grief involving deceased father (business executive); client's chronic pattern of seeking professional approval and playing caretaker role

chart_reveals.label: Transference formulation
chart_reveals.value: Consulting offer may represent enactment of client's caretaking pattern — being the 'good son who provides' for a professional authority figure

chart_reveals.label: Countertransference risk
chart_reveals.value: Financial need may be creating unconscious pull to rationalize accepting an ethically problematic arrangement

phases.clinician_prompt: You mentioned the grief work is central. How does this situation connect to what you and your client have been working on in therapy?

questions.prompt: What is the primary ethical and professional issue at the core of this peer consultation scenario?

options.A: A competence boundary issue — the psychologist lacks adequate training in organizational consulting to accept the contract
options.B: A prohibited multiple relationship in which accepting the consulting role would create a reasonably foreseeable risk of impairment to the therapeutic relationship, exploitation, or loss of objectivity
options.C: A financial conflict of interest that is ethically manageable through informed consent and documentation
options.D: A transference enactment that, once interpreted in session, would resolve the ethical dilemma and allow the psychologist to accept the contract

questions.explanation: Under APA Ethics Code Standard 3.05, a psychologist must avoid multiple relationships when there is a reasonably foreseeable risk of impairment, exploitation, or loss of objectivity. Accepting an organizational consulting contract at a current therapy client's workplace would create overlapping roles, confidentiality conflicts, and power dynamics that pose clear and foreseeable risks. The psychologist would hold therapeutic confidences while assessing the client's colleagues and supervisors, creating an untenable dual role.

distractor_rationale.A: The psychologist reports relevant training and competencies in organizational consulting. The issue is not competence but the multiple relationship created by the specific circumstances of the referral.
distractor_rationale.C: While financial conflict is a relevant factor, the ethical concern cannot be resolved merely through informed consent. The structural overlaps between therapeutic and consulting roles create foreseeable harms that documentation alone cannot mitigate.
distractor_rationale.D: While the transference analysis is clinically astute and should be addressed therapeutically, interpreting the transference does not eliminate the structural ethical violation of entering into a multiple relationship. The ethical and clinical issues are related but distinct.

questions.prompt: In conceptualizing the psychologist's difficulty in responding to this situation, which of the following best captures the interplay of factors that complicated his professional judgment?

options.A: The psychologist has a personality-based pattern of poor boundaries that requires remediation
options.B: The psychologist's financial vulnerability created a countertransference pull that, combined with the client's transference-driven offer, produced a complementary enactment that delayed appropriate ethical decision-making
options.C: The psychologist was primarily motivated by professional ambition and rationalized the opportunity as clinically complex when the ethical answer was straightforward
options.D: The client manipulated the psychologist by exploiting knowledge of his financial difficulties to create an obligation

questions.explanation: This scenario illustrates a complementary enactment — the client's transference-driven caretaking (offering an opportunity to a perceived authority figure, replicating the pattern with his father) dovetailed with the psychologist's countertransference vulnerability (financial pressure creating an unconscious pull to rationalize acceptance). Together, these produced a mutual enactment that delayed the psychologist's typically sound ethical judgment. This formulation accounts for both the relational dynamics and the situational factors.

distractor_rationale.A: There is no evidence of a pervasive boundary problem. The psychologist has maintained a two-year therapeutic relationship appropriately and demonstrates strong self-awareness and ethical reasoning in recognizing the current dilemma.
distractor_rationale.C: The psychologist's presentation throughout the consultation demonstrates genuine ethical distress, sophisticated clinical analysis, and willingness to be self-critical — inconsistent with primary ambition-driven rationalization.
distractor_rationale.D: There is no evidence of client manipulation. The client's offer is consistent with his characterological pattern of caretaking and need for professional approval, which is a therapeutic issue, not a manipulative act.

questions.prompt: Given the supervisee's presentation — declining motivation localized to the workplace, desire for feedback, and loss of a mentoring relationship — which assessment framework would be most appropriate to structure your understanding of her experience?

options.A: Maslach Burnout Inventory (MBI) to assess emotional exhaustion, depersonalization, and personal accomplishment
options.B: Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory to distinguish hygiene factors from motivators in her work environment
options.C: PHQ-9 to screen for a major depressive episode
options.D: Holland's RIASEC model to assess person-environment occupational fit

questions.explanation: Herzberg's Two-Factor Theory is most directly applicable here. The supervisee's distress centers on absent motivators (recognition, professional growth, meaningful feedback) rather than dissatisfaction with hygiene factors (salary, physical conditions). Her functioning outside work is intact, and her primary complaints map onto Herzberg's motivator dimension — achievement, recognition, and the work itself.

distractor_rationale.A: While the MBI could capture some burnout symptoms, the supervisee does not describe emotional exhaustion or depersonalization with clients; her presentation is more specifically about absent motivational factors than classic burnout syndrome.
distractor_rationale.C: The PHQ-9 screens for depression, but the supervisee explicitly reports intact functioning, sleep, social engagement, and exercise outside of work, making a depressive episode less likely and this tool less targeted to her workplace-specific concern.
distractor_rationale.D: Holland's RIASEC model assesses vocational personality-environment fit, but the supervisee reports strong alignment with her field's mission ('I went into this field to help people'); the issue is organizational, not occupational mismatch.

In my culture, you respect the hierarchy — the person in charge earned that position, and you don't question them publicly. So when staff push back in meetings, it feels… disrespectful, honestly. I know that's probably not how everyone here sees it, but it's hard to turn that off.

chart_reveals.label: Cultural leadership schema
chart_reveals.value: Hierarchical authority model rooted in family-of-origin and cultural norms; perceives staff disagreement as disrespect

chart_reveals.label: Insight trajectory

phases.clinician_prompt: I'm curious about where your ideas about what a leader should be come from.

questions.prompt: The consultee's leadership approach appears heavily influenced by culturally rooted schemas about hierarchy and authority. As the organizational consultant, which of the following approaches best addresses this cultural dimension while supporting leadership development?

options.A: Advise the consultee to adopt a fully participative leadership style, as research consistently shows it produces better outcomes across all organizational contexts
options.B: Explore how his cultural values around hierarchy can be preserved as personal strengths while also developing skills in collaborative communication that align with the organization's culture
options.C: Refer the consultee for individual psychotherapy to address the rigidity of his culturally based authority schema
options.D: Normalize the consultee's approach entirely as a culturally valid leadership style and redirect focus to the staff's need to adapt to his management

questions.explanation: Culturally responsive consultation honors the consultee's values while helping him expand his behavioral repertoire. Option B integrates cultural humility by validating his hierarchical framework as meaningful rather than pathological, while simultaneously developing skills that address the organizational mismatch. This approach avoids both cultural erasure and uncritical cultural relativism.

distractor_rationale.A: This ignores the cultural context entirely and imposes a single leadership model as universally superior, which reflects cultural insensitivity and oversimplifies leadership research that shows effectiveness is often context-dependent.
distractor_rationale.C: The consultee's cultural schema is not a clinical pathology requiring psychotherapy; framing it as such pathologizes cultural values and misidentifies the consultation target, which is leadership skill development rather than personality change.
distractor_rationale.D: While this validates the consultee's culture, it does so at the expense of ignoring the real organizational impact (35% turnover, HR complaints) and places the entire burden of adaptation on staff, which is neither realistic nor ethically sound consultation practice.

questions.prompt: To structure an evidence-based leadership development plan for this consultee, which assessment approach would be most useful as a starting point?

options.A: Administer the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory-3 (MMPI-3) to identify personality pathology contributing to leadership difficulties
options.B: Use the Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire (MLQ) with 360-degree feedback from staff, peers, and the director
options.C: Conduct a structured diagnostic interview to rule out narcissistic personality disorder
options.D: Administer the Organizational Culture Assessment Instrument (OCAI) to the entire department to determine cultural misfit

questions.explanation: The Multifactor Leadership Questionnaire (MLQ) measures transformational, transactional, and laissez-faire leadership behaviors and is specifically designed for leadership development contexts. Combined with 360-degree feedback, it provides the consultee with concrete, multi-source data on his leadership impact — information he currently lacks. This directly targets the consultation need.

distractor_rationale.A: The MMPI-3 is a clinical personality and psychopathology measure, not a leadership assessment tool. Using it in this context pathologizes a leadership development issue and falls outside the scope of organizational consultation.
distractor_rationale.C: A diagnostic interview for narcissistic personality disorder is clinically inappropriate here. The consultee's behavior is better understood through leadership style and cultural context rather than personality pathology; this approach would likely damage the consultation alliance.
distractor_rationale.D: While the OCAI assesses organizational culture, the consultation was requested specifically to address this manager's leadership impact. Starting with a department-wide culture assessment avoids the primary consultation question and may delay needed intervention.

encounter.setting: Virtual peer consultation group via telehealth platform
encounter.referral_context: Peer consultation group for early-career psychologists; member requested time to discuss a professional dilemma affecting their job satisfaction and clinical work

phases.dialogue: Thanks for letting me take some time today. So, I've been at this group practice for about two years now, and honestly it started off great. Good clients, decent autonomy, supportive partners. But over the last six months, they've been pushing me to take on more and more evaluations — forensic stuff, custody cases — and it's not what I was hired for. I'm a therapist. That's what I'm good at. But when I bring it up, they say 'we need everyone to be flexible.' And I keep thinking, flexible is one thing, but this feels like it's becoming my whole job and I didn't sign up for this.
behavioral_tags: looks away from camera when mentioning practice partners

chart_reveals.label: Presenting concern
chart_reveals.value: Role drift from psychotherapy to forensic evaluation work over past 6 months; increasing dissatisfaction and sense of professional misalignment

chart_reveals.label: Organizational response
chart_reveals.value: Practice leadership frames role expansion as 'flexibility'; dismisses concerns about scope

phases.clinician_prompt: Go ahead — we've got the time. What's been going on?

phases.dialogue: I didn't get any real training in forensic work during my program. I had one class, and it was more like an overview. The partners keep saying I'll learn as I go, but last month I had a custody evaluation where both attorneys were grilling me on my methodology, and I honestly didn't know if what I was doing was up to standard. I went home that night feeling like a fraud. My wife said I looked like I'd seen a ghost. The thing is, I don't want to seem like I can't handle it — there's an unspoken thing in the practice that the people who take on the hard cases are the ones who move up.
behavioral_tags: voice lowers when discussing competence concerns

chart_reveals.label: Training background
chart_reveals.value: One graduate course in forensic psychology; no supervised forensic practicum; no post-doctoral forensic training

chart_reveals.label: Competence concern
chart_reveals.value: Questioned during custody evaluation about methodology; reports significant self-doubt regarding adequacy of forensic skills

chart_reveals.label: Practice culture
chart_reveals.value: Implicit norm that career advancement requires taking on high-complexity cases regardless of training background

phases.clinician_prompt: When you say they want you to be flexible — what does that actually look like day to day?

phases.dialogue: Here's what really gets me. I love doing therapy. When I'm sitting with a client and something clicks for them — that's why I do this. Those moments keep me going. But now I'm spending twenty hours a week on eval reports I don't feel qualified to write, and I barely have energy left for my therapy clients. My satisfaction with work has just tanked. And it's bleeding into everything — I'm irritable at home, I'm not sleeping well, I keep second-guessing myself. I even looked at job postings last week, which I never thought I'd do. I just don't know if saying no to the evals means saying no to having a future here.

chart_reveals.label: Job satisfaction trajectory
chart_reveals.value: Initially high satisfaction tied to therapy work; marked decline coinciding with role shift to forensic evaluations

chart_reveals.label: Spillover effects
chart_reveals.value: Irritability at home, sleep disruption, self-doubt, exploring alternative employment

chart_reveals.label: Motivational pattern
chart_reveals.value: Intrinsic motivation (mastery, meaning in therapy) being displaced by extrinsic pressure (advancement contingent on compliance with role expansion)

phases.clinician_prompt: What does the work that feels meaningful to you actually look like — versus what's been taking over?

questions.prompt: The consultee describes a practice culture where career advancement is implicitly tied to taking on complex cases regardless of training. As a peer consultant, which of the following responses best addresses the intersection of organizational culture, ethics, and the consultee's distress?

options.A: Reassure the consultee that on-the-job learning is standard in psychology and that his concerns about competence are likely driven by imposter syndrome
options.B: Encourage the consultee to quietly decline forensic cases by citing scheduling conflicts to avoid direct confrontation with practice leadership
options.C: Help the consultee articulate the ethical obligation to practice within areas of competence (APA Ethics Code 2.01) and explore strategies for discussing boundaries with practice leadership
options.D: Advise the consultee to immediately file an ethics complaint against the practice for pressuring unlicensed-level forensic work

questions.explanation: APA Ethics Code Standard 2.01 (Boundaries of Competence) requires psychologists to provide services only within the boundaries of their competence based on education, training, supervised experience, consultation, study, or professional experience. Helping the consultee frame his concern as an ethical obligation — not personal weakness — empowers him to advocate for appropriate boundaries while navigating the organizational culture constructively.

distractor_rationale.A: While imposter syndrome is common in early-career psychologists, dismissing this consultee's competence concerns is inappropriate given his limited forensic training (one course, no supervised experience). His anxiety may be an accurate signal of genuine skill gaps, not merely self-doubt.
distractor_rationale.B: Avoidance strategies undermine professional integrity and fail to address the underlying ethical concern. They also leave the consultee vulnerable to continued role pressure without establishing clear boundaries or resolving the competence issue.
distractor_rationale.D: Filing an ethics complaint is premature and adversarial. The consultee has not yet attempted to address the issue with practice leadership, and the situation may be resolvable through direct communication, boundary-setting, and negotiation of appropriate training or referral pathways.

questions.prompt: Based on Self-Determination Theory, which combination of interventions would most effectively restore the consultee's work motivation and professional well-being?

options.A: Negotiate a higher salary for forensic evaluations to improve extrinsic reward, and enroll in a time management course to increase efficiency
options.B: Recommend the consultee leave the practice immediately and seek a therapy-only position that better aligns with his interests
options.C: Advocate for renegotiating his role to preserve autonomy in caseload composition, pursue forensic training if interested to build competence, and reconnect with a clinical community for relatedness
options.D: Suggest the consultee accept the forensic role expansion and reframe it as a growth opportunity to develop a more positive mindset

questions.explanation: Self-Determination Theory identifies autonomy, competence, and relatedness as three basic psychological needs that drive intrinsic motivation. Option C directly targets all three: renegotiating caseload composition restores autonomy, pursuing appropriate training builds genuine competence, and engaging with a clinical community supports relatedness. This approach addresses the root motivational disruption rather than surface-level symptoms.

distractor_rationale.A: Increasing extrinsic reward (salary) for work that undermines intrinsic motivation is unlikely to restore satisfaction per SDT research. Time management does not address the core problem of role misalignment and competence concerns.
distractor_rationale.B: While leaving the practice may eventually be appropriate, recommending immediate departure without first attempting to negotiate changes is premature and overlooks the consultee's stated desire to remain. It also fails to build skills he will need in any organizational context.
distractor_rationale.D: Telling the consultee to accept the role and reframe it dismisses legitimate ethical and competence concerns. Positive reframing without structural change does not address the actual erosion of autonomy and competence that is driving his distress.

encounter.setting: Organizational consultation — conference room at a community mental health center
encounter.referral_context: The clinic director requested an organizational consultation after two senior clinicians filed formal complaints against each other and three staff members submitted transfer requests within the same month.

patient.label: Adult Female, 46
appearance_tags: clutching a folder of printed emails

phases.dialogue: I honestly don't know where to start. I've been directing this clinic for eight years and I've never seen anything like this. Two of my best clinicians — people who used to co-lead groups together — are barely speaking. Their teams have taken sides, and now I've got three people wanting to transfer out. I feel like the whole place is falling apart and I don't even understand how we got here.

chart_reveals.label: Presenting concern
chart_reveals.value: Escalating interpersonal conflict between two senior clinicians has spread to their respective teams, resulting in formal complaints and transfer requests.

chart_reveals.label: Timeline
chart_reveals.value: Conflict escalated over the past 3 months; formal complaints filed 2 weeks ago.

phases.dialogue: So about four months ago, we restructured. Administration decided to split the adult outpatient team into two — one focused on SMI and one on general outpatient.
