chart_reveals.value: Center has 28 staff; underwent rapid expansion from 12 staff over 18 months; associate director role created 14 months ago for Dr. R.M.

phases.clinician_prompt: Thank you for meeting with me, Dr. R.M. I know this is a difficult situation. Can you walk me through what's been happening from your perspective?

phases.dialogue: When I took this role, there was no real structure. No competency tracking, no formal case assignment process — the previous director just let people pick their own caseloads. So we had a licensed counselor with zero trauma training carrying five complex PTSD cases because the clients 'liked her.' I started assigning cases based on competence. I thought I was doing the right thing. And with Marcus — the other grievance — I gave him feedback about incomplete documentation during our weekly team huddle. I didn't yell. I didn't single him out, not really. I said something like, 'We all need to tighten up on notes, and Marcus, your last three intakes were missing risk assessments.' Maybe I shouldn't have named him. But people were falling behind and I was tired of sending private emails that got ignored.

chart_reveals.label: Case assignment grievance
chart_reveals.value: Dr. R.M. reassigned trauma cases from licensed counselor (L.K.) citing competence concerns; no formal competency assessment tool used; no written policy governing case assignment by complexity

chart_reveals.label: Hostile environment grievance
chart_reveals.value: Dr. R.M. provided corrective feedback to staff psychologist (Marcus) by name during group team meeting regarding incomplete risk documentation

chart_reveals.label: Organizational structure review
chart_reveals.value: No formal competency evaluation framework, no written case assignment policy, no progressive discipline protocol, supervision structure informal and inconsistent

phases.clinician_prompt: Can you tell me more about what led up to these specific incidents — the case reassignment and the team meeting feedback?

phases.dialogue: Honestly? I think part of this is that people resent me. I was a peer — we were all staff clinicians together — and then Dr. Chen promoted me. She said I was the most organized, the most reliable. But she didn't... she didn't really give me any leadership training or define my authority. People still see me as a colleague, not a supervisor. When I try to enforce standards, it feels like I'm overstepping some invisible line nobody drew for me. And now Dr. Chen is distancing herself from the whole thing. She told the board she was 'unaware of any problematic management practices,' which... that hurt. She knew exactly what I was doing. She asked me to tighten things up.

chart_reveals.label: Role transition
chart_reveals.value: Promoted from peer clinician to associate director 14 months ago; no formal leadership training, mentorship, or role definition provided

chart_reveals.label: Organizational dynamics
chart_reveals.value: Clinical director (Dr. Chen) reportedly directed R.M. to implement standards but now denies awareness of specific management decisions; role authority boundaries unclear

chart_reveals.label: Board involvement
chart_reveals.value: Board considering leadership restructuring; has not yet initiated formal investigation; external consultation requested by clinical director

phases.clinician_prompt: It sounds like there's a lot more going on than just these two incidents. What's the broader dynamic been like since you stepped into this role?

phases.dialogue: I pulled together some things I thought you'd want to see. Here's the email chain where Dr. Chen asked me to 'handle the documentation problem however you see fit.' And here's my draft competency matrix — I was trying to build one, but it was never formally adopted. I also brought the original job description for my role, which basically just says 'assist with clinical operations.' I know it's vague. Everything here is vague.

chart_reveals.label: Email documentation
chart_reveals.value: Email from Dr. Chen to R.M. (7 months prior): 'Handle the documentation problem however you see fit — I trust your judgment.' No further written guidance provided.

chart_reveals.label: Job description review
chart_reveals.value: Associate director job description contains no explicit supervisory authority, no disciplinary powers, no case assignment authority; reads as administrative support role

chart_reveals.label: Draft competency matrix
chart_reveals.value: R.M. created informal competency tracking tool mapping clinician credentials to case complexity levels; never formally reviewed or adopted by leadership

phases.clinician_prompt: Do you have any documentation — emails, policies, your job description — that might help clarify how your role and authority were defined?

questions.prompt: Based on the full organizational consultation, what is the PRIMARY systemic issue driving the current crisis at this community mental health center?

options.A: Inadequate role definition and authority delegation, creating a role ambiguity conflict in which the associate director was given informal responsibility without formal organizational power or structural support
options.B: An interpersonally aggressive management style by the associate director reflecting poor emotional intelligence and a need for individual executive coaching on feedback delivery
options.C: Failure of the clinical staff to accept legitimate hierarchical authority, reflecting an organizational culture of entitlement and resistance to accountability
options.D: Absence of a competency evaluation framework, making the associate director's clinical judgment about case assignments the sole actionable problem requiring remediation

questions.explanation: The central organizational dysfunction is a classic role ambiguity problem compounded by inadequate authority delegation. Dr. R.M. was promoted from peer to supervisor without formal role definition, leadership training, explicit supervisory authority, or written policies to support her mandate. The clinical director informally delegated responsibility but did not codify authority, and then distanced herself when conflict arose. This structural failure — not individual incompetence — is the primary driver of the grievances.

distractor_rationale.B: While R.M.'s public feedback to Marcus was a tactical error, it reflects inadequate leadership training rather than a characterological management deficit. Framing this as solely an interpersonal skills problem misses the systemic role ambiguity and lack of organizational infrastructure that set her up to fail.
distractor_rationale.C: This frames staff as the problem and ignores that R.M. lacked formal authority. Staff resistance is a predictable organizational response to unclear hierarchical transitions, not evidence of entitlement. The grievances may reflect legitimate confusion about R.M.'s actual scope of authority.
distractor_rationale.D: The absence of a competency framework is indeed a contributing factor, but it is a symptom of the broader systemic failure — the organization failed to build infrastructure to support the new leadership role. Focusing solely on the competency tool misses the role ambiguity, authority delegation, and leadership accountability issues.

questions.prompt: In differentiating between the contributing organizational factors, which of the following would MOST help determine whether the associate director's actions constituted genuine scope-of-practice violations versus appropriate clinical management hampered by structural deficits?

options.A: Administering a standardized burnout inventory to all staff to assess whether workplace stress levels are elevated across the organization
options.B: Reviewing whether the associate director holds appropriate credentials and licensure to make clinical supervision and case assignment decisions within the state's regulatory framework
options.C: Conducting anonymous staff satisfaction surveys to determine whether the majority of employees share the grievants' perceptions of the work environment
options.D: Evaluating whether the clinical director's informal directives to the associate director created an implied scope of authority that was organizationally sanctioned, even if undocumented

questions.explanation: The critical differential in this organizational case is whether R.M. was acting outside her authority (a true scope violation) or acting within an informally sanctioned but poorly documented scope of authority that the organization failed to formalize. Evaluating the clinical director's directives, email documentation, and the organization's de facto power structure would most directly clarify this distinction and determine organizational versus individual accountability.

distractor_rationale.A: While burnout assessment provides useful climate data, it does not differentiate between whether R.M.'s specific actions were within or outside her legitimate authority. It addresses workplace wellness broadly but not the specific differential question of scope violations versus structural failure.
distractor_rationale.B: Reviewing R.M.'s credentials and licensure is important for due diligence but is unlikely to resolve the core question. The grievances are about organizational authority (whether she had the role-based power to reassign cases and deliver corrective feedback), not about whether she holds appropriate clinical credentials. She is already licensed as a psychologist.
distractor_rationale.C: Staff satisfaction surveys capture climate perceptions but do not resolve the factual question of whether R.M.'s actions were authorized. Majority perception does not determine whether organizational authority was legitimately delegated.
